cards.body: Remember 'your grandmother' from Chapter 1? Time to learn her real name. Welcome to the Filipino family.
Nanay means 'mom.' Some families say 'Mama' or 'Mommy' in Taglish, but nanay is the Filipino word.
Tatay means 'dad.' You'll also hear 'Papa' or 'Daddy' in Taglish families.

example.note: Greeting + po + family name. Natural and respectful.

cards.source_text: How do you greet your mom in the morning?
cards.explanation: Magandang umaga po, Nanay! Parents get po. Always.

cards.body: Anak means 'child' or 'son/daughter.' It's what your parents call you.

example.note: The most Filipino parent sentence ever.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: Filipino parents call their kids 'anak' regardless of age. You could be 40 and your nanay still calls you anak.

cards.source_text: Your mom calls you to dinner. She says: '___,  kumain ka na!'
cards.explanation: Anak = child. What Filipino parents call their kids.

cards.source_text: Match the word to its meaning.
cards.explanation: Tatay = dad. Nanay = mom. Anak = child.

cards.prompt: Greet your dad respectfully in the evening.
cards.explanation: Magandang gabi po, Tatay! Good evening, Dad.

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue. User must get ALL questions right to complete.
wrong_answer_tracking.wrong_count_usage: Feed to Gemini system prompt for AI Practice.

alam_mo_ba: In the Philippines, 'Nanay' and 'Tatay' are also used to address elderly strangers as a sign of respect, even if they're not your parents.
Many Filipino families mix Taglish for parents: 'Mom' and 'Dad' in conversation, 'Nanay' and 'Tatay' in formal or emotional moments.
Anak literally means 'offspring.' There's no separate word for son or daughter in Tagalog. Anak covers everyone.
